category: stale meta description contradicting current page body

consistency.notes: All non-null/non-empty ground-truth fields are supported by the page text or embedded variant data. 'coffee.roaster_country' is supported indirectly by 'Portland, Oregon' plus free US shipping context, and 'is_specialty_coffee' is supported by the specialty roaster/product context.

realism.reasons:
Plausible overall as a real roaster product page: the structure is very standard Shopify-style coffee PDP text extraction, with nav, breadcrumbs, price, size/grind selectors, stock note, tasting notes, farm/process details, brewing recommendation, shipping note, related products, variant block, and footer. The coffee details are coherent and current-feeling: Colombia / Huila / Bruselas / El Diviso / Nestor Lasso / Ombligon / natural anaerobic is a believable specialty setup, and the flavor language fits that profile. The variant data also looks realistic in format and pricing, with sensible SKUs and a credible 250g-to-1kg price step.

Main tell lowering it from a 5: the meta description is for a completely different coffee ('Kenya Gichathaini AA') and does not match the page title or body. That kind of mismatch can happen on real sites, but it reads like a synthetic artifact here. A smaller tell is the slightly polished, generic marketing copy ('desired moisture and water activity were reached'), which is believable but a touch templated. Also, only one visible size is shown in the UI while the embedded variant block includes 1kg; not impossible in html-to-text extraction, but mildly awkward. Still, aside from those inconsistencies, this is quite close to a real specialty roaster page.

label.coffee.sensory_text:
Tasting notes: raspberry jam, pink grapefruit, cacao nib, rose

This release from El Diviso is all about saturated fruit and a polished, perfumed finish. The cup opens with raspberry jam and pink grapefruit before settling into cacao nib and a gentle rose-like florality as it cools.
label.coffee.display_tasting_notes: raspberry jam, pink grapefruit, cacao nib, rose
label.coffee.producer_text: Nestor Lasso and the team at El Diviso have become known for careful, repeatable fermentation work in Huila. For this Ombligon selection, ripe cherries were hand sorted, held in a sealed fermentation environment, and then dried slowly as a natural lot until the desired moisture and water activity were reached.
